items.text: Writing a book is a horrible, exhausting struggle, like a long bout of some painful illness.
items.context: Orwell describing the physical and mental ordeal of writing in his essay on the motivations for prose writing
items.publicReaction: Widely quoted by writers and scholars as an honest assessment of the writing process
items.source: Why I Write, Gangrel magazine, Summer 1946

items.text: It was in Burma, a sodden morning of the rains. A sickly light, like yellow tinfoil, was slanting over the high walls into the jail yard.
items.context: Opening of Orwell's essay describing the sensory experience of witnessing an execution in colonial Burma
items.publicReaction: The passage became famous for its vivid imagery and emotional precision in conveying the atmosphere of capital punishment
items.source: A Hanging, The Adelphi magazine, August 1931

items.text: Within certain limits, it is actually true that the less money you have, the less you worry.
items.context: Orwell reflecting on poverty from his personal experience living as a vagrant and in workhouses
items.publicReaction: Sociologists and historians cited this as Orwell's paradoxical insight from his time living in poverty
items.source: Down and Out in Paris and London, Chapter 3

items.text: good prose is like a windowpane
items.context: Orwell's metaphor for clear writing—prose should be transparent and not call attention to itself
items.publicReaction: Became a foundational principle in writing education and literary criticism
items.source: Why I Write, Gangrel magazine, Summer 1946
